did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a dermoscopically informed deep learning model using a dual-branch Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture with an EfficientNetB0 backbone to classify actinic keratosis (AK) versus cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) in situ from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and evaluating a novel dermoscopically informed deep learning model (dual-branch CNN with EfficientNetB0) for classifying actinic keratosis and cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma. It reports specific methodology including dataset size (2,000 images), training details, and performance metrics (accuracy 98.61%, sensitivity 98.33%, specificity 98.90%) derived from their own experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Dermoscopically informed deep learning model for classification of actinic keratosis and cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma
brief_summary: This study developed a dermoscopically informed deep learning model to classify actinic keratosis (AK) versus cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) in situ using dermoscopic images. The researchers utilized a dual-branch Convolutional Neural Network architecture with an EfficientNetB0 backbone, integrating targeted preprocessing to enhance vascular and keratinization patterns alongside standard RGB inputs. The model was trained and evaluated on a dataset of 2,000 images sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive, expanded via augmentation to nearly 200,000 instances. Performance was assessed using repeated hold-out validation across 10 iterations, yielding an accuracy of 98.61%, sensitivity of 98.33%, and specificity of 98.90%. These results surpassed previously reported models for this specific classification task, demonstrating that clinically informed preprocessing significantly improves CNN performance. The authors conclude that this approach offers a robust tool to support dermatologists in differentiating between AK and cSCC with greater confidence.

lesion_types_studied.details: Premalignant skin lesion; 1,000 images used in the dataset for binary classification against cSCC in situ.
Malignant neoplasm; 1,000 images used in the dataset for binary classification against AK. The study explicitly focuses on the in situ subtype.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category: Actinic Keratosis (AK) versus Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma (cSCC) in situ
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model is designed to 'classify AK versus cSCC in situ' using dermoscopic images. It reports binary classification metrics including accuracy (98.61%), sensitivity (98.33%), specificity (98.90%), precision (98.90%), and F1-score (98.61%). The model uses categorical cross-entropy loss, indicating a discrete class prediction task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive (via Kaggle subset)
datasets_used.description: A curated subset of dermoscopic images derived from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Archive, an open-access dermatologic imaging repository. Images were provided in JPEG format in RGB color space with an original resolution of 600 × 450 pixels, rescaled to 500 × 500 pixels for training.
classes_used: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma in situ (cSCC)

use.training: Used for training the dual-branch CNN model. The dataset was expanded via geometric augmentation (horizontal flipping) to 4,000 images and deep learning-based augmentation (MixUp) to expose the model to nearly 200,000 training instances across 50 epochs. Data was split 80% for training and 20% for testing.
use.validation: Used for validation via repeated hold-out validation across 10 iterations.
use.testing: Used for testing (20% of the dataset).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To obtain a more robust evaluation of model performance by randomly splitting the dataset into training and testing sets ten times, training and evaluating the model independently on each split, and averaging the performance metrics to mitigate bias from a single partition.
To measure model performance by summarizing the number of correct and incorrect predictions for each class (AK and cSCC) and calculating percentages of correctly classified samples per class.
To report final performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score) by averaging the results obtained from the 10 iterations of the repeated hold-out validation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on descriptive statistics (averaged metrics from repeated hold-out validation) and comparative analysis against state-of-the-art models without formal significance tests.

ISIC Archive (AK vs cSCC in situ).experiment: CNN model with data augmentation and preprocessing with repeated hold-out validation
ISIC Archive (AK vs cSCC in situ).notes: The model was evaluated using repeated hold-out validation across 10 iterations. The confusion matrix for the repeated hold-out approach showed 7889 correct predictions out of 8000 samples (98.32% for AK and 98.89% for cSCC). The single-split hold-out confusion matrix showed 789 correct predictions out of 800 samples (98.71% for AK and 98.53% for cSCC).

State-of-the-art comparison.notes: The proposed model's performance (98.61% accuracy, 98.33% sensitivity, 98.90% specificity) surpassed all listed state-of-the-art approaches.

architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNetB0 backbone was pre-trained (weights implied by 'pre-trained' description in text, though specific dataset like ImageNet not explicitly named in the provided chunks, standard for EfficientNetB0). The lightweight convolutional branch for extra channels was not pre-trained.
architecture_used.input_size: 500 × 500 pixels (rescaled from original 600 × 450 pixels). Input tensor consisted of 3 RGB channels plus 2 additional channels generated through targeted preprocessing to enhance vascular and keratinization patterns.

architecture_details.secondary_branch: Lightweight convolutional branch for the two additional preprocessed channels
architecture_details.fusion: Features from both branches were concatenated

training_details.data_augmentation: Geometric augmentation (horizontal flipping) and deep learning-based augmentation (MixUp)
training_details.dataset_size: 2,000 images (1,000 per class), expanded to nearly 200,000 training instances
training_details.validation_strategy: Repeated hold-out validation across 10 iterations (80% training, 20% testing split)

classification_or_output_head.details: Concatenated features from the dual branches were passed through a dense head for classification

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Dual-branch Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with EfficientNetB0 backbone
CNN model without data augmentation or preprocessing
CNN model with data augmentation only
CNN model with data augmentation and preprocessing (single split)
CNN model with data augmentation and preprocessing with repeated hold-out validation
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images rescaled from original 600 × 450 pixels to 500 × 500 pixels prior to model training.
Targeted preprocessing for feature enhancement: Generation of two additional channels to enhance vascular and keratinization patterns, integrated alongside original RGB inputs.
Geometric augmentation: Horizontal flipping to double dataset size.
Deep learning-based augmentation: MixUp technique for creating interpolated virtual samples on-the-fly.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal flipping was applied to the original dataset of 2,000 images to double the dataset size to 4,000 images.
MixUp technique was used to create interpolated virtual samples on-the-fly, exposing the model to nearly 200,000 training instances across 50 epochs.

conclusions: Incorporating clinically informed preprocessing to enhance vascular and keratinization patterns significantly improves the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for classifying actinic keratosis (AK) versus cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) in situ.

The proposed dermoscopically informed dual-branch CNN model achieved superior performance (accuracy 98.61%, sensitivity 98.33%, specificity 98.90%) compared to previously reported models for this specific binary classification task.

Data augmentation techniques provided the largest individual performance gain (approximately 8% across metrics), while the addition of targeted preprocessing channels further increased performance by approximately 6%.

Repeated hold-out validation across 10 iterations provided a more robust and less biased evaluation of model performance compared to single-split hold-out validation, confirming the model's robustness.

The study demonstrates that moving beyond raw pixel data to include structured dermoscopic features can address limitations in generalizability and real-world applicability associated with standard 'black-box' AI models.
This approach represents a step toward clinically aligned AI systems capable of supporting dermatologists in differentiating between AK and cSCC with greater confidence and precision.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small dataset size (2,000 images) and a narrow scope (only AK and cSCC in situ), which may affect generalizability to larger, more diverse clinical populations or other histological subtypes like invasive cSCC.

The model was evaluated exclusively on retrospective dermoscopic images from a public repository (ISIC Archive) without uniform acquisition standards, introducing variability in image quality; prospective, multicenter clinical validation is needed.

There is a risk of 'Clever Hans' behavior, where the model might rely on spurious background cues or acquisition artifacts rather than lesion-specific features, as explainability methods like Grad-CAM were not included in this study.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to compare the proposed model against state-of-the-art baselines, relying instead on descriptive metric averaging.

The manuscript is an unedited 'Article in Press' version, which may contain errors affecting content, and IRB approval/patient consent were marked as N/A, indicating reliance on de-identified public data.
Performance metrics in the abstract (loss 0.3120) differ from those in the results section (loss 0.1320), indicating potential inconsistencies in the unedited manuscript.

The model's superior performance (98.61% accuracy) is claimed over prior studies, but these comparisons are against heterogeneous multi-class skin lesion classification tasks rather than direct binary AK vs. cSCC benchmarks, limiting the validity of the comparison.
